title: Local Intertwining Relation
speaker: Sug-Woo Shin
abstract: The local intertwining relation (LIR) is an identity that gives precise information about the action of normalized intertwining operators on parabolically induced representations. It plays a central role in Arthur’s endoscopic classification for quasi-split classical groups. In this talk, I will discuss some seed theorems on LIR needed to complete Arthur's work, focusing on one of them: how to extend LIR from the tempered case to the case of non-tempered Arthur packets via Aubert duality (a.k.a. Aubert-Zelevinsky involution). Based on joint work with Hiraku Atobe, Wee Teck Gan, Atsushi Ichino, Tasho Kaletha, and Alberto Minguez.

proof_stages.label: Endoscopic-classification setting and postponed local ingredients
proof_stages.summary: The talk situates the local intertwining relation inside Arthur--Mok endoscopic classification for quasi-split classical groups and identifies Arthur's postponed assertions 8.25--8.27, especially 8.25, as the local technical target.

proof_stages.label: Classical-group inductive architecture
proof_stages.summary: Using the symplectic group as a model, the speaker explains how parabolic induction, ordinary endoscopy, and twisted general linear groups combine to make an induction on rank plausible for quasi-split classical groups.

proof_stages.label: Packets, component-group labels, and endoscopic character relations
proof_stages.summary: The needed local objects are set up: tempered L-parameters, Arthur A-parameters, packets of representations, component-group pairings, and the endoscopic character relation comparing stable linear combinations after transfer.

proof_stages.label: Aubert duality and cotempered parameters
proof_stages.summary: The Aubert--Zelevinsky involution on representations is paired with the elementary operation of swapping the two SL_2 factors of an A-parameter, giving a mechanism that exchanges tempered and cotempered parameters.

proof_stages.label: Place of 8.25 in Arthur's induction
proof_stages.summary: Arthur's induction first proves local theorems for tempered parameters, then uses 8.25 to pass to cotempered packets, which is sufficient input for the subsequent global argument yielding the general non-tempered case.

proof_stages.label: Cotempered packets and corrected ECR pairing
proof_stages.summary: For cotempered psi, the packet is defined as the Aubert dual of the tempered packet for the dual parameter, and the main problem is to define the unique component-group pairing satisfying ECR, including a nontrivial sign correction controlled via signed Aubert duality, supercuspidal support parity, and rho-derivatives.

proof_stages.label: Local intertwining relation statement
proof_stages.summary: For a cotempered parameter induced from a Levi subgroup, the normalized local intertwining operator attached to the relevant Weyl/component-group element is defined, and LIR asserts that on each irreducible constituent it acts by the corresponding endoscopic sign.

proof_stages.label: Proof of LIR by reduction and gamma-factor comparison
proof_stages.summary: The proof reduces to maximal Levi subgroups and then to one constituent, compares the induced-representation intertwiner with the standard-module intertwiner, and computes the remaining ratio of normalizing factors through local gamma-factor calculations.

examples.note: Shimura varieties are mentioned as a place where quasi-split classical groups naturally arise, illustrating why endoscopic classification is useful beyond the local theorem itself.

examples.name: Trivial and Steinberg representations under Aubert duality
examples.note: The mutual Aubert duality of the trivial and Steinberg representations illustrates that Aubert duality can take a tempered representation to a non-tempered one, the phenomenon exploited later.
examples.engagement_reason: The trivial and Steinberg representations are invoked as a known Aubert-dual pair illustrating that temperedness need not be preserved.

passes.reason: The trivial representation is a collapsed base case and an extreme non-tempered example paired with Steinberg to show Aubert duality need not preserve temperedness.

The Steinberg representation being Aubert-dual to the trivial representation shows temperedness can fail to be preserved, while the trivial representation is minimal and described as most nontempered.
